extra.reasoning:
rec_drug_a is set true by exactly one rule, rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50), whose condition is (asthma AND qt_prolongation AND g6pd_deficiency AND pregnancy). No other rule touches rec_drug_a, so rec_drug_a is true ONLY under that full four-way conjunction; otherwise the default leaves it false.

rec_drug_b is affected by two rules: rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50, IF asthma THEN true) and guard_suppress_b (priority 90, IF the same four-way conjunction THEN false). The guard's condition is identical to the condition that makes rec_drug_a true.

Therefore, in every input where rec_drug_a becomes true, the four-way conjunction holds, so both rec_b_from_trigger (wants true, prio 50) and guard_suppress_b (wants false, prio 90) match for rec_drug_b. Per priority conflict resolution, the highest-priority matching rule wins: guard_suppress_b at priority 90 beats rec_b_from_trigger at 50, forcing rec_drug_b := false. Hence whenever rec_drug_a = true, rec_drug_b is necessarily false.

When the conjunction is false, rec_drug_a = false (default), so there is no risk of both being true regardless of rec_drug_b. There is no input assignment making (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b) jointly true. The mutual-exclusion property holds over the entire input space.
